turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Hi, thanks for having me today.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Great to meet you. To start, could you give us a quick self-introduction?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Sure — I'm a senior backend engineer with about ten years of experience building distributed systems, most recently at large-scale platforms in the search and storage space.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Walk me through your most recent role — what you owned and the team setup.

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: I was a senior engineer on the indexing platform team, about twelve people, owning the ingestion pipeline that handled billions of documents per day.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Roughly how many years have you been working directly on distributed systems?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Around eight years focused on it, and the other two were adjacent — concurrency and storage internals.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: I noticed a project on your resume — can you tell me more about it and your specific contribution?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: I led the design of a sharded metadata service that replaced our legacy Zookeeper setup; I owned the consensus layer and the migration tooling.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: What would you say was the biggest quantified achievement at your recent employer?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Reducing p99 ingestion latency from 1.2 seconds to under 180 milliseconds while cutting infrastructure cost by 35%.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Why are you interested in this Staff role at Pillarstream specifically?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Your streaming infrastructure work lines up directly with what I've spent my career on, and the staff scope here looks like the right next step.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Based on the resume, you clearly meet our distributed-systems bar — anything you'd like to highlight there?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: I've run multi-region consensus systems in production and led a couple of postmortems on real incidents, so I'm comfortable owning that surface area end to end.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: There's one area we want to dig into — what's your hands-on experience with exactly-once semantics and event sourcing at scale?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: I've implemented idempotent producers with deduplication windows and worked on Kafka-backed event sourcing, though I'd say my depth there is moderate compared to consensus work.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Tell me about your education — degree, school, and any relevant coursework.

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: B.S. in Computer Science from a state university, graduated around 2014. Nothing outside the standard curriculum worth flagging.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Give me a STAR-format example of a tough production challenge you owned.

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Sure — Situation: a multi-region write path started failing during a deployment. Task: I was the incident commander. Action: I rolled back, traced it to a clock-skew bug in the new lease manager, and shipped a fix plus monitoring. Result: full recovery in under two hours, and we avoided it recurring the next quarter.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: What are your salary expectations for this role?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: I'm targeting the upper band for staff at this level, probably in the 240k to 280k base range depending on equity.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Do you have any questions for us?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Yeah — what does the on-call rotation look like for this team, and how is tech leadership structured across staff engineers?

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Got it — how long did the incident recovery itself take from page to all-clear?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: About 95 minutes from the first page to the all-clear, with mitigation happening earlier.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: One last thing — do you currently hold a valid driver's license?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: I do, yes — it's a standard U.S. license, currently valid.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Quick note before we wrap — our office does have a coffee bar that people seem to like.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Before we close — what does your typical week look like outside of work, and is there anything about your background we haven't covered that you'd want me to know? And what interests you most about this opportunity?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: I spend a lot of weekends on side projects, mostly distributed-systems tinkering, and I mentor a couple of early-career engineers. I think that covers the main gaps.

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Alright, thanks for your time today — we'll be in touch soon with next steps.
